verses.commentary: Second closing formula ('when Jesus had finished'). The mission discourse ends; Jesus himself continues to teach and preach. Sending the Twelve does not retire him from the field.
John in prison (Herod's fortress at Machaerus). 'Deeds of the Messiah' (ta erga tou Christou, τὰ ἔργα τοῦ Χριστοῦ) — Matthew names them messianic explicitly. John, hearing, sends disciples.
'Are you the one who is to come (ho erchomenos, ὁ ἐρχόμενος)?' Even John doubts. Prison and silence have eroded confidence in the very one he baptized. Faith does not insulate from crisis.
Jesus does not give a yes/no but evidence. 'Tell John what you hear and see.' Empirical verification — let the data settle the question.

Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 woven together — blind see, lame walk, lepers cleansed, deaf hear, dead raised, poor evangelized. The messianic checklist is being executed. But notice: no Roman expulsion. John's prison hint suggests his question's source.
Blessed is the one who does not stumble (skandalisthe, σκανδαλισθῇ) on account of me. The unexpected shape of messiahship will trip many. John needs encouragement to hold on.
After John's disciples leave, Jesus defends John publicly. 'A reed shaken by wind?' — image of vacillation. No, John was not unstable.
'A man in soft clothes?' — court luxuries are in palaces, not in wilderness. John is no compromised courtier.
'A prophet? Yes, and more than a prophet.' The introduction to the climactic identification.
Malachi 3:1 quoted: 'I send my messenger before your face.' John is the forerunner-messenger himself. Prophecy fulfilled in personnel.
Greatest born of women — superlative praise. Yet the least in the kingdom is greater. The kingdom era surpasses even the prophetic age. New thing in salvation history.
From John until now, kingdom 'suffers violence' (biazetai, βιάζεται) and violent men seize it (harpazousin, ἁρπάζουσιν). Ambiguous — either persecution or aggressive entry. The kingdom is contested.
Law and Prophets prophesied 'until John.' John is the threshold. Before him: anticipation. After him: arrival.
'If you are willing to accept it' — qualifier. John is the promised Elijah (Malachi 4:5). Reception of John's identity is itself a faith-act.
'Whoever has ears, let them hear' — invitation that doubles as filter. Hearing requires more than auditory function.
'This generation' — children playing in marketplaces, complaining their playmates won't follow their game. The generation is petulant, refusing both available scripts.
'We played the flute, you didn't dance; we sang a dirge, you didn't mourn.' Neither festivity nor lament could move them. Total disengagement masquerading as discrimination.
John ascetic — 'he has a demon.' His severity dismissed.

Son of Man eating and drinking — 'glutton and drunkard, friend of tax collectors and sinners.' His warmth dismissed too. Wisdom justified by her deeds (or 'her children' in some manuscripts) — the actual fruit vindicates the method.
Begins denouncing cities where his miracles were performed. Reception, not display, is the measured response.
Chorazin and Bethsaida — Galilean towns now lost to history. Tyre and Sidon (notorious Phoenician pagan cities) would have repented at the same evidence. Theological geography reversed.
Tyre and Sidon's judgment day more bearable. Greater exposure to revelation = greater accountability. The light unaccepted condemns more deeply.
Capernaum — Jesus' adopted town — addressed. 'Exalted to heaven? You will go down to Hades.' Even Sodom would have stood had it received such miracles. The reversal cuts to Jesus' own headquarters.
Sodom's judgment less severe than Capernaum's. Privilege without response is the gravest condition.

Jesus prays aloud. Father, Lord of heaven and earth. Hidden from 'wise and learned' (sophon kai syneton, σοφῶν καὶ συνετῶν) — the scribes, the experts. Revealed to 'little children' (nepiois, νηπίοις) — the unsophisticated.
'Yes, Father, for so it was your good pleasure.' Sovereign election affirmed without explanation. The way revelation distributes is finally the Father's choice.
Christological climax: all things delivered to the Son. Mutual exclusive knowledge of Father and Son. Revelation of Father is by Son's volition. Trinitarian intimacy declared.

'Come to me, all who labor and are burdened (kopiōntes kai pephortismenoi, κοπιῶντες καὶ πεφορτισμένοι).' Universal invitation. 'I will give you rest' (anapauso, ἀναπαύσω) — Sabbath echo, eschatological repose.

'Take my yoke and learn from me.' Yoke (zygon, ζυγόν) — rabbinic image of teacher's interpretation of Torah. Jesus' yoke is not lawless freedom but his particular teaching. 'Gentle and humble in heart' (praus kai tapeinos te kardia, πραΰς καὶ ταπεινὸς τῇ καρδίᾳ) — the teacher's character is the qualification.
'My yoke is easy (chrestos, χρηστός, kindly), my burden is light (elaphron, ἐλαφρόν).' Contrast: the scribes' yoke is heavy (compare 23:4). Jesus' Torah-reading liberates.
